A few years later, some review comments (I'm mostly looking at this from the POV of the Signer interface and  #447):

I think we should not expose all of this as a new module -- especially things like get_keys_on_hsm(), get_hsms() seem like things that should not be public API here

`HSMSigner` on the other hand sounds like a reasonable thing: The API would include 
* key and device ids as constructor arguments (optional if PyCKS supports that)
* PKCS module loaded using environment variable
* PIN as constructor variable

This would be compatible with #447 with a URI like `"hms:?slot=c9&device=0"`, and pin getting requested with `secrets_handler()`
 
The remaining functionality would then be :
* export_pubkey()

Interestingly this same issue comes up with KMS keys... Maybe _public key export_ could actually be a method on the Signer as well: not all implementations would support it but some could.

Another thing that comes up with KMS keys as well is the need to prehash the payload. We should add that support somewhere -- figuring out the algorithm from the public key is currently very annoying. Can we have `Key.get_payload_hash_algorithm()`?

Left also some code comments -- they may be obsolete so feel free to disregard

LGTM overall (should wait for others to validate the API), but just to make sure I understand the limitations of this:

1. When working with a future representing the return object of a task, future cancellation will not cancel the corresponding task, and we'd need a tighter concurrent.futures integration (e.g. Ray implementing a concurrent.futures executor) in order to support that, right?
2. In the same vein, `future.running()` will always be `False`, even if the task that produces the future is currently running.

Are there any concerns with only supporting the semantics of a subset of the `concurrent.futures.Future` API? My first impression is that this is fine for now, and that this shouldn't be blocked by a proper `concurrent.Futures` API.

I added a couple of comments inline, but the general feeling that I have is that merging this as-is could introduce potentially hard to track down bugs, especially related to the use of `setattr` and the changes to caching mechanisms.

My proposal is following:
1. Let's remove, caching `set/getattr` tricks etc. to keep this PR as simple as possible. A single I/O request or a few is not a big deal in the big picture, and we can always come back to that after we have a clean, working solution.
2. We clean up the vacuumcontainers by moving the code into feature-specific files (i.e., by the command that is used to get the data). This will make it not only easier to maintain but easier to add new features.
3. We find a way to introduce caching, if it still makes sense.

As I'm not seeing any tests or payloads for the new information here, I have no idea about what would be the best way forward on this. When/if you find time, ping me on discord and let's have a synchronous chat/call to figure this all out :-)

I think it would be worthwhile adding a code comment here that explains that `c.session.Accept()` blocks until `c.session.Close()` is called, which happens during `c.Close()`, but `c.Close()` itself also locks `c.m`, so we have to unlock `c.m` while `c.session.Accept()` is running, in order to avoid the deadlock.

We could mention that this deadlock was first discovered in go1.19 when https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/409537 started enforcing that listener go routines complete before active connections are cleaned up after a server close / shutdown, and that prior to this, we leaked deadlocked go routines without noticing.

I think it would be worthwhile adding a code comment here that explains that `c.session.Accept()` blocks until `c.session.Close()` is called, which happens during `c.Close()`, but `c.Close()` itself also locks `c.m`, so we have to unlock `c.m` while `c.session.Accept()` is running, in order that `c.Close()` isn't blocked, which would otherwise cause a deadlock. We could mention that this deadlock was first discovered in go1.19 when https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/409537 started enforcing that listener go routines complete before active connections are cleaned up after a server close / shutdown, and that prior to this, the deadlocks would still have been happening, leaking deadlocked go routines, but functionality would not have been impacted, only performance.

Hey Brian,

I checked the full history of `taskcluster-proxy` in the monorepo, and `taskcluster-proxy` in its original repository, and this appears never to have been the behaviour of `taskcluster-proxy` in the past.

TLDR; I think this is the wrong place to make the fix.

I think whatever broke, broke somewhere else, and this fix may end up causing us more pain than it solves. For example, it looks like this will set the `Content-Type` header for requests that have no body, such as GET requests, which could introduce adverse affects in middleware or caching proxies etc. I think passing through only the headers that are sent, plus the Authorization header, is the correct approach, and I suspect whatever was calling taskcluster-proxy is the thing that broke, rather than taskcluster-proxy itself.

I realise this might sound like I'm splitting hairs, but it feels like this change is just to mask problems elsewhere and could lead to unexpected results. For example, calling code that doesn't set the header when it runs through the proxy, wouldn't work if it was pointed at the taskcluster endpoint directly. The clients should all set the `Content-Type` header when it is required, so if it is getting missed somewhere, we have a different problem that this change would mask.

Thanks - this looks great. We should keep backwards compatibility with dicts or at least raise an actionable DeprecationWarning.

The conversion back to a scalingconfig data class is too specific to be included in Ray Tune's resolve logic. In my opinion there are two possible solutions here:

1. The scaling config is converted to a dict before passing to Tune, and back to a data class in the trainer before calling the self.training_loop. This would be in-line with the current logic, and the specific `scaling_config` key is in-scope of the Ray AIR trainer
2. Alternatively, we can introduce a `tune.sample.TuneableDataclass` abstraction and have `ScalingConfig` inherit from this. Ray Tune would then check for these classes in the original spec, convert them into dicts, and convert them back into their respective dataclasses afterwards. This is more involved and possibly out of scope of this PR.

I'm happy with either solution but believe that 1) is less invasive and can be done quickly.

@Irving-cl I don't think we should focus on the option about providing link metrics information from the last frame. That's against the spec, so the only option to support Thread 1.2 would be to provide link metrics information for the acknowledged frame.

As for supporting this in the current nRF platform, I believe we would only need a way to temporary store the LQI and RSSI when processing the received frame (`nrf_802154_received_timestamp_raw`) and retrieve that information when filling the IE data (`nrf_802154_tx_ack_started`).

We can probably forget about `OT_RADIO_CAPS_AUTO_ENH_ACK` as you suggested. When multicore platform support is added, all this can be handled by its `radio.c`, with no new changes in OpenThread implementation itself.

Do you think this is achievable?

Hi @usamamuneeb! Thanks again for making this PR.

The main thing that jumps out to me is the need for more documentation. You can look at PR #1682 to see that you need to update ``doc/source/install/index.rst`` and ``doc/source/tutorial/advanced/index.rst``. When you update those files, make sure to provide links to the [sdpa-python GitHub repo](https://github.com/sdpa-python/sdpa-python) and its [proper web documentation](https://sdpa-python.github.io/).

The other thing that stands out to me is the difficulty of installing this SDPA support. This is relevant because we try to have all solver interfaces tested in our continuous integration pipelines. PR #1655 added support for the GLOP solver and updated our CI infrastructure by changing a single line in ``continuous_integration/install_dependencies.sh``. I think Installing SDPA by building from source would take prohibitively long for us to run it on all platforms. But it would be nice for us to test it on Linux for some version of Python.

That said, rather than updating CVXPY's CI in this PR, I'd prefer if you could make a GitHub Actions pipeline *on the sdpa-python repo* that demonstrates how to successfully build sdpa-python with OpenBLAS and run its examples. If you make such a pipeline then I can port your code to CVXPY's CI with appropriate conventions/style.

```

I'm not sure changing the dictionary order is a good way to handle this. While it will work and it's fine in CPython, pure python itself doesn't guarantee the order of a dictionary, dicts are supposed to be an inherently unordered data type, and in other implementations of python, such as Pypy, iterating through the dict may result in a different order than the one in which the dict was defined.

Even in CPython it's not guaranteed that this current ordered behavior of dicts will be preserved in future versions. Instead, it may be wise to use `collections.OrderedDict`, or an approach with 2 separate dictionaries, checking the first one before the other one. In fact, in previous python versions, this was not the case and dict order was essentially random.

i think the main problem with the approach is state inconsistency this will cause in the `ServicePluginManager`.

currently, the `ServiceManager` is never really used, it's always the `ServicePluginManager`, (which btw already has a `stop_services` method that we should probably reconcile).
any deletion of service state in the `ServiceManager` base class needs to be reconciled with the underlying state of loaded plugins specs by the `ServicePluginManager`, otherwise there will be state incosnstency. for example, `list_available` of the `ServicePluginManager` uses the list of loaded plugins, rather than the list of service containers like the `ServiceManager` base class. so if we only delete the services from the `_services` dict, `list_available` of `ServicePluginManager` will still return the services.
unfortunately, there is currently a certain finality to loading plugin specs, as it reads them from the cache of entry points, and also loads them into a cached property in the `ServicePluginManager`, the `api_provider_specs`.

another thing to consider is that starting services can currently have significant side effects to the system (handlers being added, patches being applied, etc...), and we haven't been disciplined about making sure that services properly undo those side effects.

it would be helpful to better understand the requirements, and what the problem is exactly. is this for something that has to do with state restoring? so are we trying to work around some service state problem? do we need to fully re-load services? or is this just to remove all services that haven't been started from some state data?

I think the cost aspect of s3 is fine, but there's a larger downside: we can't write to it from PRs from forks. This means the data doesn't include a large batch of data, and, in particular, doesn't include the flakes that affect humans most embarrassingly (discouraging new/irregular contributors: us maintainers/people with push access have already overcome that hurdle). I assume the rate of flakes is similar/the distribution is the same, but it still seems unfortunate to drop that data...

However, the alternative is GHA artifacts, which are hard to query and have a retention time, after which they're deleted. Neither of these are complete blockers... but they certainly don't make GHA a slam-dunk.

Something is better than nothing, and neither option is obviously better than the other, so more than happy to go with S3 and iterate.

Thanks for implementing this, @DaelonSuzuka !

I didn't think about it before, but we need to set `__cause__` on the error, so we can:

* Actually indicate that that it was an attribute access that was the proximate cause of the error, and inform the user which attribute required PyOpenGL
* Give the details of the ImportError/ModuleNotFound error that happened on import, giving them useful information about what actually happened rather than leaving them (and us or whomever they report it to) guessing whether the module is missing, there's a DLL issue, it was installed/uninstalled incorrectly, it was pip/conda mixing, etc.

Therefore, after a bunch of experimentation, since this added a lot of logic to every call site, I ended up putting this in a helper utility function and and calling that in `__getattr__`.

I was originally going to make this as suggestions, but it turned out to be rather complicated, and prevent putting the common logic into a utility function somewhere else. Thus, I ended up just pushing it to [a branch on my fork](https://github.com/CAM-Gerlach/qtpy/tree/improve-import-hygiene) you can pull with

```bash
git remote add cam-gerlach https://github.com/CAM-Gerlach/qtpy.git
git fetch cam-gerlach
git pull cam-gerlach improve-import-hygiene
```

Or, I can push it to your branch directly, if you prefer. Thanks!

Thoughts:

- I agree the current state of things potentially breaking as soon as something calls context.instance is not great.

- Requiring the user code server to be able to load the instance seems like a reasonable requirement to me - I think we used to have that, and removed it when we split the helm chart out into a user code helm chart and a system helm chart. With the benefit of hindsight I think allowing user code to work without an instance there was probably an error since you need to be able to load the instance anyway in order to do lots of things. Loading the instance once on user code server startup and passing it through / always being able to assume that it exists and has loaded already would be a lot simpler then all these lazy-loaded instance ref shenanigans (and probably more performant too - right now I believe we are creating a separate DagsterInstance object on every tick).

- it would be easier to guarantee that the instance will always load if we moved to a UserCodeLauncher model where dagster is responsible for spinning up the user code servers and setting all the needed secrets on them, this is how cloud works already today.

- I don't actually think optional args is the core problem here, that may be a problem for other reasons but the arg was set in the case where it broke for the user, it just didn't load when it instantiated because a secret was missing.

- the other angle you could take here is threading the lazy-loading even further down into the resource generation code, and have it wait to load the instance until there's a resource that actually needs to access the instance in its initialization function (I would guess that these are fairly rare). But that is probably doubling down on a bad setup.

- Overall I think we should land this and have a plan for a future where the instance is loaded once always on gRPC server startup

Hey @harshithakolipaka, welcome and great to see you improving our documentation! I've left a few suggestions. Please feel encouraged to ask if something is not clear to you. :slightly_smiling_face: 

I am a bit surprised that you chose to add docstrings to our test suite. Files and functions that start with `test_` are usually part of our test suite and not of our official API. It's not a bad thing to add them here, however, it's also not strictly necessary. :wink: 

However, I think it would be useful to add these docstrings to our ["Adapting gray-scale filters to RGB images" tutorial](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/a4558e13c1b6db7d88d55f4a71fd97702a079427/doc/examples/color_exposure/plot_adapt_rgb.py). That tutorial uses `adapt_rgb` in a similar manner. 

You are referencing #6761. Perhaps the description of that issue is a bit confusing. The goal of #6761 is not to add docstrings to every function anywhere, but to add it to each submodule. In Python that means, adding a docstring at the beginning of each `__init__.py` file. You can see an example of this here https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/a4558e13c1b6db7d88d55f4a71fd97702a079427/skimage/io/__init__.py#L1-L5

Hello @osirisinferi ! Looking at your PR, I see that you basically introduced a dedicated new output function for the JSON format, that, as for the plain text format, takes the internal Python objects to generate the appropriate output.

I am a little woried that this design may create a maintainance overhead that could lead to some inconsistencies in the internal logic of the two functions, and so to the related outputs.

So I have an alternate design that I think could reduce this risk. Basically, I think that `certbot certificate` should always generate the JSON representation of the certificates from Certbot internal objects, then, depending on the flags passed:
* if `--json` is passed, output the JSON directly
* if not, call the "plain text" function, and generate the output *from the JSON*.

This way, the two outputs are strongly coupled and consistency is stronglier ensured.

What do you think ?

> I've rejiggled the work distribution. Now Windows, macOS and normal (x86_64 and i686) Linux get one job each. Then the really slow qemu emulation (Linux on aarch64) is split per Python version. Overall runtime is 13 minutes with Windows being the slowest (despite have half as much work to do as the native Linux runner 🙄).

What do you think about also splitting the `deploy-wheels-native.yml` matrix into Python versions? Then each one would be maybe 90 seconds or less.

Then the bottleneck would be a QEMU job at around 9 minutes, but all the others should have finished by then.

Another nice benefit of separate jobs is each wheel is uploaded to PyPI a few minutes earlier, and so narrowing the window someone ends up getting and building the sdist.

---

Thanks for bringing up this point! The PyTorch documentation for [CUDA semantics](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/notes/cuda.html#tensorfloat-32-tf32-on-ampere-devices) mentions that `allow_tf32` defaulted to `True` until version 1.12. Instead of having a flag for this, we could preserve the default behavior by always setting `torch.backends.cuda.matmul.allow_tf32 = True`. Users would get the performance improvements without needing to update their Sockeye configurations when updating PyTorch.
